87fd855e-fe3a-4550-a017-f7822971c44b.document:
The National Center of Excellence for Veterans and Their Families at Rush University Medical Center. Provides comprehensive mental health care including the Accelerated Treatment Program (ATP) for PTSD, TBI, and MST; intensive outpatient services; couples counseling; child counseling; caregiver support groups; survivor counseling; and the 'Do You Love a Vet' support group (1st Tuesday monthly, 6-7:30 PM). All services at NO COST regardless of discharge status. Serves veterans of ALL eras, service members, and families. Family defined as: spouses, partners, children, parents, siblings, caregivers. Illinois & Indiana: full outpatient services. PSYPACT states (43 states): Virtual Accelerated Treatment. Nationwide: Do You Love a Vet program and annual in-person ATP.

Eligibility: Veterans of all eras, service members, and their families regardless of discharge status

Cost: Free - no cost for any services

Additional Contact: 1645 W. Jackson Blvd., Suite 602, Chicago, IL 60612

2ad3102a-f581-47af-a245-8ddc70070f51.document:
24/7 crisis support for veterans, service members, and their families. If you or someone you know is in crisis, reach out immediately. Trained counselors are available around the clock.

Eligibility: All veterans, service members, and their families

Cost: Free

Additional Contact: Text: 838255 | Chat online at veteranscrisisline.net

f470fae5-6be4-47fa-b80a-25b61224a524.document:
Free, confidential counseling and support services for active-duty, National Guard, Reserve service members, and their families worldwide, 24/7. Offers 12 free confidential counseling sessions per issue; face-to-face, phone, or online video counseling; specialty consultations (financial, legal, education); spouse education and career support; Military and Family Life Counselors (MFLCs) at installations.

Eligibility: Active-duty, National Guard, Reserve service members and families

Cost: Free

4987e571-4b53-4b36-9fb2-aaf468a0b104.document:
Located at Emory University in Atlanta, Georgia. Part of the Warrior Care Network. Provides two-week intensive outpatient treatment program, individual and group therapy, wellness workshops and mindfulness training, family participation opportunities. All treatment, travel, meals, and related expenses covered 100%.

Eligibility: Post-9/11 veterans and service members

Cost: Free - all expenses covered

18c841fb-be2a-4656-ba95-f9f027fe4eaa.document:
Located at UCLA in Los Angeles, California. Part of the Warrior Care Network. Offers accelerated brain health treatment, PTSD/TBI/MST specialized care, cohort-based treatment model (healing alongside fellow veterans), art therapy and collaborative activities. No-cost program covering all expenses.

Eligibility: Post-9/11 veterans and service members

Cost: Free - all expenses covered

7b03baa4-f433-4168-b7ca-5db8cfc7ec4d.document:
Located at MGH in Boston, Massachusetts. Part of the Warrior Care Network. Delivers comprehensive mental health assessment and treatment, two-week intensive treatment program, evidence-based therapies by veteran mental health specialists, peer-to-peer support and education. All costs covered. Also serves pre-9/11 veterans and their families.

Eligibility: All era veterans and service members

Cost: Free - all expenses covered

3924bacd-38de-4517-83f5-4815da67c817.document:
Specialized counselors providing free, confidential, non-medical counseling for military children and youth on installations, schools, and child development centers. Topics: deployment stress, anger management, bullying, self-esteem, sibling relationships, family dynamics.

Eligibility: Military children and youth

Cost: Free

The oldest LGBTQ+ veterans organization in the US, founded in 1990. Provides advocacy, peer support, community connection for LGBTQ+ veterans. A member of the Road Home Program Outreach Team serves as President of the AVER Chicago Chapter.

fa3a0012-50b2-4846-a291-f85d238e4621.document:
Source: 2025 Blue Star Families Military Family Lifestyle Survey

- 40% of active-duty families face waitlists for mental health care
- 44% experience waitlists for child mental health care
- 90% on child mental health waitlists waited 3+ months
- 28% of military families experience food insecurity
- Military spouse unemployment: 23% vs 4.4% nationally
- Only 44% report satisfaction with military life
- 68% say two incomes vital to financial stability

b834df6c-5e72-426c-921a-2015fdb1310f.document: The Road Home Program at Rush University Medical Center is a leading center for veterans and service members seeking treatment for invisible wounds of war including post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD), traumatic brain injury (TBI), military sexual trauma (MST), anxiety, depression, and related conditions. The program provides world-class clinical care at no cost to veterans and their families regardless of discharge status or era of service. Founded in 2014 with support from the Wounded Warrior Project, the Road Home Program is part of the Rush University Medical Center system in Chicago, Illinois. The program offers intensive outpatient treatment, family services, and research to improve care for those who served.

f7dbbc7d-2173-4a1d-a480-ac2973e42d13.document: The Road Home Program at Rush University Medical Center provides world-class care for veterans and service members struggling with the invisible wounds of war. The program was established in 2014 at Rush University Medical Center with a foundational grant from the Wounded Warrior Project. It has since treated thousands of veterans from across the country. The program uses evidence-based treatments including Cognitive Processing Therapy (CPT) and Prolonged Exposure (PE) therapy for PTSD. The Road Home Program is committed to removing barriers to care for veterans, including providing treatment at no cost, eliminating the need for VA referrals, and welcoming veterans regardless of discharge status, era of service, or branch. The program is part of Rush University Medical Center, a premier academic medical center in Chicago.

edae0c85-fab3-48c2-b6be-bdaee8f8812f.document: The Road Home Program Family Center provides comprehensive support services for families of veterans and service members. The center recognizes that the invisible wounds of war affect the entire family, not just the service member. Family services include family therapy, couples counseling, parenting support, psychoeducation about PTSD and TBI, and support groups. The Family Center offers both in-person and telehealth options. Services are available to family members even if the veteran is not enrolled in the Road Home Program. The center helps families understand the impact of military service, develop communication skills, build resilience, and strengthen family relationships.

805d390e-0aef-479d-91b4-ed8855a54cc8.document: Contact the Road Home Program at Rush University Medical Center. Phone: (312) 942-8387. The Road Home Program is located at Rush University Medical Center in Chicago, Illinois. Veterans and family members can call to learn more about services, schedule an intake assessment, or ask questions about the program. The intake process begins with a phone call to the Road Home Program. Staff are available to answer questions and guide veterans through the enrollment process.

826516de-6586-47b3-9ac0-3e1db98a1044.document: The Road Home Program offers several evidence-based treatment programs for veterans and service members. The Intensive Outpatient Program (IOP) is a 3-week program that provides concentrated treatment for PTSD using evidence-based therapies including Cognitive Processing Therapy (CPT) and Prolonged Exposure (PE) therapy. Veterans attend daily sessions and receive comprehensive care including individual therapy, group therapy, and complementary wellness activities. The program also addresses co-occurring conditions such as TBI, depression, anxiety, substance use, and military sexual trauma. Outpatient services are available for veterans who need ongoing care or who may benefit from a less intensive treatment schedule. The Road Home Program also offers specialized treatment tracks and programming tailored to the unique needs of veterans. All treatment is provided at no cost to the veteran.

e47ff750-d3f9-4a7c-8182-161012ee286e.document: Getting care at the Road Home Program is straightforward. The first step is to call (312) 942-8387 to speak with a member of the intake team. The intake team will conduct a phone screening to learn about the veteran's experiences and needs. Based on the screening, the team will recommend the most appropriate treatment track. Veterans do not need a referral from the VA or any other provider. The program serves veterans from all eras of service and all branches of the military, regardless of discharge status. There is no cost for treatment. Travel and lodging assistance may be available for veterans traveling from outside the Chicago area. The program welcomes veterans from across the country.

dade6cbc-4ee1-4173-b106-e1e7acbd7412.document: The Road Home Program is committed to advancing the science of treating invisible wounds of war through research. The program conducts clinical research to evaluate treatment outcomes and improve care for veterans with PTSD, TBI, and related conditions. Research areas include treatment efficacy, neuroimaging, biomarkers, and novel therapeutic approaches. The program collaborates with researchers at Rush University Medical Center and other institutions. Research findings are published in peer-reviewed journals and presented at national conferences. The program's research contributes to the broader understanding of PTSD and TBI treatment and helps inform evidence-based practices nationwide. Veterans may have the opportunity to participate in research studies during their treatment.

c2ecd8e3-1e97-4e71-9d7a-eb3f4b501888.document: Support the Road Home Program through various giving options. Donations help fund treatment services for veterans at no cost. Ways to give include online donations, planned giving, corporate partnerships, and fundraising events. The Wounded Warrior Project is a major supporter of the program. All donations go toward providing treatment and support services for veterans and their families. The Road Home Program is part of Rush University Medical Center, a 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization, making donations tax-deductible.

metadata.title: Ways to Give - Road Home Program

dbc66314-e7f5-4bed-a67c-375527b72e7c.document: The Road Home Program team consists of experienced clinicians, researchers, and support staff dedicated to serving veterans and their families. The clinical team includes psychiatrists, psychologists, social workers, and other mental health professionals with expertise in treating PTSD, TBI, and related conditions. Team members are trained in evidence-based therapies including Cognitive Processing Therapy and Prolonged Exposure therapy. The program leadership oversees clinical operations, research initiatives, and outreach efforts. Many team members have specialized training in military culture and the unique challenges faced by veterans.

4b084961-4ac0-468e-a87a-74c95886952a.document: The Road Home Program provides a collection of resources for veterans, service members, and their families. Resources include crisis hotlines, partner organizations, educational materials about PTSD and TBI, and links to additional support services. Key crisis resources: Veterans Crisis Line: 988 (press 1) or text 838255. National Suicide Prevention Lifeline: 988. Crisis Text Line: text HOME to 741741. Additional resources include the VA, Wounded Warrior Project, Give an Hour, Cohen Veterans Network, and other organizations serving veterans.
